current_fact.fact_text: On April 3, 2000, a U.S. federal judge ruled in United States v. Microsoft Corp. that Microsoft had illegally maintained a monopoly, famously saying the company kept "an oppressive thumb" on competitors. The case exposed how something as intangible as a bundled web browser could reshape global markets and ideas of fair play.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

current_fact.match_reason: A landmark antitrust case about software giants shaping our digital world pairs naturally with an episode about our fraught relationship to the internet and connected devices.

queue.fact_text: In 1969, U.S. Defense Secretary Melvin Laird announced the policy of "Vietnamization": gradually withdrawing American troops from the Vietnam War while training and equipping South Vietnamese forces to take over the fighting. It was a strategy born from political pressure at home and the realization that military dominance doesn’t automatically translate into sustainable peace.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Trip-hop’s murky, cinematic soundscapes match the ambiguity and psychological weight of Vietnamization—a war shifting shape rather than simply ending.

queue.fact_text: On April 3, 2010, Apple released the first-generation iPad, a tablet computer that many critics initially dismissed as a "big iPhone" without a keyboard. Yet it helped define a new device category between phones and laptops, shifting how people read, watch, and make media on the move.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

queue.match_reason: The first iPad transformed how we carry music and media, echoing this episode’s nostalgia for earlier portable devices and the evolution from MP3 players to tablets.

queue.fact_text: In 2008, Texas authorities raided the FLDS’s remote YFZ Ranch after reports of underage marriage and abuse, ultimately removing 533 women and children into state custody. The event spotlighted how closed religious communities can collide with modern legal systems over questions of consent, power, and protection.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: This clash between a secretive religious sect and outside law enforcement mirrors flamenco’s themes of repression, sorrow, and defiance, carried in raw, emotionally charged vocals.

queue.fact_text: Also on April 3, 1996, after a nearly two-decade manhunt, authorities arrested Theodore "Ted" Kaczynski—the Unabomber—at his tiny off-grid cabin in Montana. Inside, they found a typewriter, homemade bombs, and thousands of handwritten pages outlining his anti-technology manifesto.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Kaczynski’s violent rejection of modern technology contrasts sharply with an episode about embracing and critiquing a new vocal technology, Auto-Tune—both centering on how tech reshapes human expression.

queue.fact_text: In 1975, reigning World Chess Champion Bobby Fischer refused to defend his title under FIDE’s rules, forfeiting his crown to challenger Anatoly Karpov without a single move being played. The biggest match of the decade became the most famous non-match in chess history, a story of genius tangled with obsession and control.

episode.title: Morning Drops #53 - Tyler, The Creator
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin follows the evolution of Tyler, the Creator, from the kid nobody took seriously to one of the most important artists of his generation. From a cockroach eaten on camera as a joke to Grammy stages shared with his mother, this episode traces a career built on stubbornness, reinvention, and the quiet courage of refusing to stay in one place.

Listen, move, and let no one put you in a box.

Tracklist:
Tyler, the Creator – Like Him (feat. Lola Young)
Tyler, the Creator – Yonkers
Tyler, the Creator – See You Again (feat. Kali Uchis)
Tyler, the Creator – Earfquake
Tyler, the Creator – Ring Ring Ring

queue.match_reason: Fischer’s refusal to play by the rules parallels Tyler, The Creator’s refusal to fit into any single box; both turned their fields upside down through stubborn, individualistic choices.

queue.fact_text: On April 3, 1961, LAN-Chile Flight 621 struck a mountain in the Andes, killing all 24 people onboard, including Argentine footballer Eliseo Mouriño. The crash underscored how clouds, rugged terrain, and early navigation limits made flying over the world’s longest mountain range as perilous as it was spectacular.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A plane lost in the Andes connects with an episode built around mountain landscapes, where music and stories echo the beauty and danger of high places.

queue.fact_text: The Himalaya’s crown, Mount Everest—overflown for the first time on this date in 1933—stands at 8,848.86 meters above sea level and grows a few millimeters each year as the Indian Plate continues to push into Eurasia. Even today, its summit is high enough that climbers enter the “death zone,” where human bodies slowly fail without supplemental oxygen.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: Everest invites thoughts of thin air and deep presence at the planet’s roof, fitting an episode about immersion, altered states, and moving through vast, elemental spaces.

queue.fact_text: The Cold War—context for Truman’s Marshall Plan—was a global standoff from the late 1940s to the early 1990s that never became a full nuclear war, but came dangerously close. From Berlin to Cuba to space itself, it turned ideology into an arms race, while everyday people lived under the shadow of weapons designed never to be fired.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: The Cold War’s vast, tense "journey" across decades and continents fits with a wide-ranging, atmospheric voyage through genres and moods, like a sonic map of a divided world.

queue.fact_text: Modern tornado science leans heavily on the Fujita scale, introduced in the 1970s by Ted Fujita to rank tornado intensity from F0 to F5 based on damage. Fujita pieced together wind speeds by examining splintered houses, bent steel, and even grass scoured from the ground, essentially using destruction as a forensic ruler.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Turning wreckage into a scientific scale is a story of learning from failure and damage, echoing this episode’s gentle exploration of persistence, experimentation, and growth through mistakes.

queue.fact_text: When mobile phones first appeared in the 1980s, they were bulky car-bound status symbols; by the time of the first iPad’s release in 2010, most people carried pocket-sized computers more powerful than 1960s supercomputers. The history of mobile phones is really a story about shrinking hardware and expanding expectations of constant connection.

episode.title: Morning Drops #26 - Canserbero
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the story of Canserbero, the Venezuelan rapper who reshaped Latin American hip hop with his raw voice and deeply reflective lyrics. A journey into his music, his vision, and the legacy of an artist who transformed personal and social struggles into powerful storytelling.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como una película y su tragedia, comedia y ficción

queue.match_reason: The evolution of mobile phones reshaped how artists like Canserbero are heard and shared; his socially conscious rap travels on the same networks that grew out of that technological history.

published.fact_text: In 1933, two biplanes from the British Houston–Mount Everest Flight Expedition became the first aircraft to fly over Mount Everest, climbing into the thin, unpressurized air with open cockpits and oxygen masks that sometimes froze. Funded by eccentric millionaire Lady Houston, the mission was as much a feat of engineering as of human stubbornness, bringing back the first close-up aerial photographs of the world’s highest peak.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the Everest overflight and Arctic whale dives are extreme, cold, high-risk expeditions into Earth’s most forbidding places, mirrored by the episode’s cinematic, awe-filled tone.

published.fact_text: On April 3, 1974, the Super Outbreak unleashed 148 tornadoes across 13 U.S. states and parts of Canada in less than 24 hours, one of the most violent storm barrages ever recorded. Entire towns were scarred, with simultaneous F4 and F5 tornadoes carving paths so wide that meteorologists had to invent new ways to describe the chaos.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The intense, violent energy of a massive tornado outbreak pairs with an "Electric Shock" set full of roaring guitars and storm-like, high-voltage riffs.

published.fact_text: In 1948, U.S. President Harry S. Truman signed the Marshall Plan into law, sending about $5 billion in its first year to help rebuild 16 war-torn European countries. Beyond money, it exported an idea: that economic cooperation and rebuilding infrastructure could be a more powerful defense than any weapon.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'é Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: The Marshall Plan is about rebuilding a fractured Europe, and this Italian-focused episode travels across a once-devastated continent’s cultural rebirth through songwriting and regional contrasts.

published.fact_text: On April 3, 2007, a modified French TGV train set a world speed record for conventional rail, streaking across the LGV Est line at 574.8 km/h (357.2 mph). The train ran so fast that the overhead wires and pantograph had to be redesigned so the electricity could keep up with it.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A record-breaking bullet train belongs with an "On The Go" travel mix whose grooves feel like motion, acceleration, and endless tracks ahead.

published.fact_text: In early April 1942, Japanese forces launched a fierce assault on U.S. and Filipino troops holding out on the Bataan Peninsula, after months of siege and starvation. The defenders’ stubborn resistance delayed Japan’s advance in the Pacific, at a terrible human cost later symbolized by the Bataan Death March.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A desperate, prolonged stand in wartime Bataan echoes with the themes of resistance, sacrifice, and political struggle that Rage Against The Machine embodies.

published.fact_text: In 1980, the U.S. Congress restored federal recognition and a trust relationship with the Shivwits, Kanosh, Koosharem, Indian Peaks, and Cedar City bands of the Paiute people of Utah. After decades of "termination" policies that tried to erase their legal status, this legislation acknowledged survival and sovereignty instead.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: The Paiute bands’ recognition and the Tuareg story both speak of Indigenous peoples, land, exile, and endurance, mirrored by desert blues steeped in survival and identity.

published.fact_text: On April 3, 1973, engineer Martin Cooper made the first handheld mobile phone call while standing on a New York sidewalk, dialing a rival at Bell Labs from a prototype that weighed over a kilogram. That clunky device—the DynaTAC—sparked a transformation that turned phones from fixed objects into constant companions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The first mobile phone call is the genesis of our modern phone obsession, which this episode dissects emotionally and psychologically through stories and tech-tinged tracks.

published.fact_text: In 1956, an F5 tornado tore across western Michigan in the Hudsonville–Standale tornado, shredding homes so completely that investigators later used the wreckage to help calibrate the highest category of the Fujita scale. Debris like asphalt ripped from roads and large objects tossed hundreds of meters showed just how violent nature’s small spinning columns can be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating tornado born from storm clouds pairs with an episode that meditates on rain, weather, and the emotional storms we outlast with hope.

published.fact_text: On April 3, 1885, Gottlieb Daimler received a German patent for a compact, high-speed four-stroke engine that he soon mounted on a wooden-framed frame called the Reitwagen, effectively creating the first gasoline-powered motorcycle. The contraption looked more like a motorized bicycle with training wheels, but it quietly launched a new way to move.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: Daimler’s engine turned mechanical rhythm into motion, just as this episode traces machines turning rhythm into dance—both about engines, speed, and mechanical futures.

published.fact_text: In 1996, a U.S. Air Force Boeing T-43 carrying U.S. Commerce Secretary Ron Brown and 34 others crashed into a mountainside near Dubrovnik, Croatia, while attempting a non-precision approach in poor weather. The tragedy exposed how navigation procedures, terrain awareness, and politics intersect in the invisible pathways aircraft follow.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A fatal crash in the hills above Dubrovnik invites sober reflection on fragility and recovery, themes mirrored by an episode about healing from injury and looking back at difficult turning points.

published.fact_text: On April 3, 1989, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians v. Holyfield that tribal courts, not state courts, had jurisdiction over an adoption involving Choctaw children under the Indian Child Welfare Act. The decision affirmed that tribal sovereignty extends even beyond reservation borders when it comes to protecting Native families.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: A legal battle over who gets to protect Native children resonates with an episode honoring powerful voices who challenge injustice and affirm identity, especially within families and communities.

published.fact_text: In 1721, Robert Walpole quietly became Britain’s first de facto Prime Minister, though the title didn’t officially exist and he publicly rejected it. By controlling cabinet appointments and managing Parliament from behind the scenes, he effectively invented a role that would shape modern parliamentary democracy.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: Walpole’s unofficial "episode one" of the prime ministership aligns with Sedna’s own first broadcast—both origin points where something powerful begins almost informally.

published.fact_text: In 2013, record-breaking rainfall over just a few hours turned parts of La Plata and Buenos Aires into sudden lakes, killing over 50 people and flooding homes up to their ceilings. The deluge overwhelmed 19th-century drainage systems that had never been designed for a climate this intense.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A city overwhelmed by water suggests the need for a "Slow Planet"—an episode that literally slows the tempo, inviting reflection on climate, pace, and how we live with nature’s extremes.

published.fact_text: On April 3, 2000, a U.S. federal judge ruled in United States v. Microsoft Corp. that Microsoft had illegally maintained a monopoly, famously saying the company kept "an oppressive thumb" on competitors. The case exposed how something as intangible as a bundled web browser could reshape global markets and ideas of fair play.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A landmark antitrust case about software giants shaping our digital world pairs naturally with an episode about our fraught relationship to the internet and connected devices.
